did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and hybrid models, specifically a proposed CNN-LSTM-SVM hybrid model, to diagnose multi-class skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset. It compares the performance of these AI models against dermatologists for skin cancer classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a proposed CNN-LSTM-SVM hybrid model for multi-class skin cancer diagnosis using the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (88.24% accuracy) and compares these results against other deep learning models and dermatologists, which constitutes original research rather than a literature review.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Deep Learning and Hybrid Models to Diagnose Multi-Class Skin Cancer
journal: 2023 International Conference on Next-Generation Computing, IoT and Machine Learning (NCIM)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of multi-class skin cancer diagnosis by comparing the performance of various deep learning and hybrid models against dermatologists. Utilizing the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 skin lesion images across seven categories, the authors evaluated several architectures including customized CNNs, transfer learning models like ResNet50, and proposed hybrid models such as CNN-SVM, CNN-Random Forest, and CNN-LSTM. The primary evaluation approach involved measuring accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores to determine the most effective classification method. The results demonstrated that the proposed CNN-LSTM-SVM hybrid model achieved the highest accuracy of 88.24%, outperforming other deep learning models and the baseline dermatologist accuracy range of 62% to 80%. Consequently, the study concludes that the CNN-LSTM-SVM model is a highly suitable and effective tool for detecting different classes of skin cancer.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for multi-class skin cancer classification.
One of the seven categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for multi-class skin cancer classification.
One of the seven categories in the HAM10000 dataset; noted as having the least amount of data (1.1% of total).

One of the seven categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for multi-class skin cancer classification.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin cancer diagnosis (7 categories: actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevi, basal cell carcinoma, vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'multi-class classification of skin cancer' using the HAM10000 dataset. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (classes) rather than continuous values. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A standard dermatological image dataset consisting of 10,015 samples of skin lesions. It is a public dataset frequently used in machine learning networks for cancer detection. The dataset is noted for being highly imbalanced, with a significant fraction belonging to the Melanocytic nevi class and the dermatofibroma (df) class accounting for only 1.1% of the total.

use.training: Used for training the proposed customized CNN, CNN-SVM, CNN-Random Forest, CNN-LSTM, and CNN-LSTM-SVM models, as well as transfer learning models (ResNet50, DenseNet121, VGG19). K-fold validation was performed, with four folds utilized due to space limitations.
use.validation: Used for validation via K-fold cross-validation (4 folds).
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of the implemented models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for model validation and performance evaluation.
Used to balance the imbalanced dataset by generating synthetic points for minority classes.
Used to scale the data features to reduce sensitivity to outliers and handle significant differences in feature ranges.
Used to expand the dataset and balance skewed data classes, particularly for underrepresented classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The CNN-LSTM-SVM hybrid model achieved 88.24% accuracy, surpassing other deployed models in this study. The paper references ResNeXt101 achieving 93.2% accuracy in a cited study [4] and EfficientNet B4/B5 achieving 87.95% in another cited study [2]. The accuracy of dermatologists is reported as ranging from 62% to 80%.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported for the proposed model; pre-trained models (VGG19, ResNet50, DenseNet121) were used for comparison.
architecture_used.input_size: 600x450 pixels (HAM10000 dataset images scaled to fit training models)

architecture_details.cnn_layers: Five convolutional layers, each followed by a batch normalization layer and a max-pooling layer
architecture_details.dropout: 0.05 for LSTM layers, 0.20 for recurrent dropout
architecture_details.dense_layers: Three dense layers with ReLU activation functions
architecture_details.classifier: Support Vector Machine (SVM) layer at the end
architecture_details.feature_processing: Data flattened through Fully Connected (FC) layer before SVM

training_details.data_balancing: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) and Data Augmentation (ImageDataGenerator)

classification_or_output_head.principle: Margin maximization to differentiate classes in high-dimensional feature space

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation using ImageDataGenerator to balance skewed data classes
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) to harmonize the dataset and balance class distribution
Image resizing/scaling to fit model input requirements (images scaled to fit training models)
Minimal pre-processing strategies adopted due to the highly skewed and large nature of the HAM10000 dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to artificially expand the dataset and balance skewed data classes. The expansion rate was set to 300%. Data augmentation was not applied to the melanocytic nevi (nv) class as it already had a reasonable amount of data (67% of the dataset).
Used to equalize the size of data across all classes and harmonize the dataset by generating synthetic points for minority classes.

conclusions: The proposed CNN-LSTM-SVM hybrid model achieved the highest accuracy of 88.24% in multi-class skin cancer diagnosis, outperforming other deployed deep learning and hybrid models in this study.
The CNN-LSTM-SVM model demonstrated superior performance compared to the baseline accuracy range of dermatologists, which was reported as 62% to 80%.

The study highlights that multi-class skin cancer classification is challenging due to narrow variances between classes and dataset imbalance, necessitating techniques like SMOTE and data augmentation.

While the proposed hybrid model performed well, the paper notes that highly complex models do not always generate the best outcomes, citing EfficientNet B4 and B5 as examples of intermediate complexity models that performed well (87.95% accuracy) in related work.

The CNN-LSTM-SVM architecture, combining feature extraction from a custom CNN, sequential modeling via bidirectional LSTM, and classification via SVM, proved to be a highly suitable and effective tool for detecting different classes of skin cancer.
important_remarks: The study relies on the HAM10000 dataset, which is highly imbalanced (Melanocytic nevi constitutes a significant fraction, while Dermatofibroma is only 1.1%), necessitating the use of SMOTE and data augmentation to balance classes.

The paper acknowledges that multi-class skin cancer classification is challenging due to narrow variances between classes, leading to mediocre results for both deep learning models and dermatologists compared to binary classification.

The proposed CNN-LSTM-SVM model achieved 88.24% accuracy, but this result is compared against a reported dermatologist accuracy range of 62% to 80%; the specific methodology for deriving this dermatologist baseline is not detailed in the provided context.
The authors note that highly complex models do not always yield the best outcomes, citing EfficientNet B4 and B5 (87.95% accuracy) as examples where intermediate complexity performed well.
The study utilized only 4-fold cross-validation due to space limitations, which may affect the robustness of the performance evaluation compared to standard 5-fold or 10-fold splits.

The paper references external studies for comparative metrics (e.g., ResNeXt101 at 93.2% from [4] and EfficientNet variants from [2]), rather than re-running these specific models on the same data split for direct comparison within this work.
The dataset includes images from diverse skin tones, but the paper does not report stratified performance analysis by skin tone, which is a known bias factor in dermatological AI.
The input image size for the proposed models was reduced to 64x64 pixels, which may result in loss of fine-grained diagnostic details compared to higher-resolution inputs used in other studies.
